chunk_summary: This section discusses resource-adapted imaging, antibiogram use, and referral pathways for bone infections in India, emphasizing practical diagnostic sequences, local microbiology, and timely referral in resource-constrained settings.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of resource-adapted imaging techniques like plain radiographs, ultrasound, and MRI in diagnosing bone infections in India.
Learn the criteria for early referral and management in district hospitals for pediatric bone infections.
Recognize the importance of local antibiograms and microbiology in guiding empiric therapy.
Identify key pathogens and resistance patterns relevant to bone infections in India.
key_facts: Plain radiographs are the first-line imaging modality to exclude fractures, tumors, and bony destruction.
MRI is prioritized for uncertain diagnoses, pelvic or spinal disease, multifocal symptoms, abscesses, or preoperative mapping.
Referral is recommended if no clinical improvement occurs within 48 hours or if large collections or neurologic deficits are suspected.
Kingella kingae is likely underdetected in India due to insensitive isolation methods, while MRSA and skeletal TB are more prevalent.
Clindamycin use for staphylococcal infections requires inducible resistance testing when available.
reference_quote: Plain radiographs remain the first test because they are widely available and help exclude fracture, tumor, and established bony destruction. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Referral criteria and pathways in resource-limited settings
